channel.title: Travel on Tanishq Srivastava
channel.description: Recent content in Travel on Tanishq Srivastava

item.title: Ladakh on Two Wheels: A Road Trip Through the Roof of the World
item.description:
There’s a particular kind of silence you only find above 4000 metres. The air is thin, the sky is impossibly blue, and the mountains stretch in every direction like they’re trying to make you feel small on purpose. Ladakh does this well.

The Route

We started from Manali, taking the classic loop:

Manali → Keylong → Sarchu → Leh
Leh → Khardung La → Nubra Valley → Pangong Lake
Pangong → Tso Moriri → Manali

Total: ~1500 km across 10 days. Two riders, two motorcycles, one tent, and an optimistic medical kit.

Day 1–2: Manali to Leh via Sarchu

The Manali–Leh highway is spectacular and exhausting in equal measure. The road climbs through five high passes — Rohtang, Baralacha La, Lachulung La, Nakee La, and Tanglang La (5328 m, the second-highest motorable pass in the world).

Sarchu sits at 4253 m and serves as the midpoint camp. We stayed in a tent hotel — basic but warm. The night sky here is one of the clearest I’ve seen anywhere; no light pollution, the Milky Way visible with the naked eye.

Altitude tip: don’t rush. Acclimatise in Manali for at least a day before heading up. Diamox (acetazolamide) is worth having, but drink water religiously.

Day 3–4: Leh

Leh is the kind of place that earns its reputation. The old town, Leh Palace perched on the hillside, and the market lane are all worth a slow morning. We spent two days here to acclimatise properly before pushing higher.

Recommended: Shanti Stupa at sunset. Get there early — the crowd fills up fast.

Day 5–6: Nubra Valley via Khardung La

Khardung La at 5359 m is the headline — often cited as the world’s highest motorable road (disputed, but the views are indisputable). The descent into Nubra is dramatic: the valley floor is green, there are sand dunes at Hunder, and Bactrian camels wander past like they own the place. Because they do.

Day 7–8: Pangong Lake

The road from Nubra to Pangong via Shyok valley is the most technically demanding section — loose gravel, river crossings, no guardrails. Worth every minute.

Pangong Tso is 134 km long, stretches into China, and changes colour through the day from turquoise to cobalt to deep blue. Photographs don’t capture it. You need to sit beside it for an hour.

Day 9–10: Tso Moriri and back to Manali

Tso Moriri is less visited than Pangong but arguably more beautiful. We had the lake almost to ourselves at sunrise. The return to Manali via the Leh–Manali highway felt faster somehow — familiarity compresses distance.

Practical Notes

Item Detail
Best season June – September
Permits ILP required for Nubra, Pangong, Tso Moriri
Fuel Fill up in Leh — sparse stations beyond
Connectivity Near zero outside Leh; download offline maps
Budget ₹3000–5000/day (fuel, food, stay)

Would I do it again?

Already planning the next loop. Spiti valley is calling.
